mappings.rationaleAI: Japan's Meiji Restoration launching rapid industrialization, militarism, and colonial expansion directly addresses the modernization of Japan and its emergence as a world power (B), including the fall of the Tokugawa and Meiji reforms (B.1).
Hong Kong's history as a British colony from 1842 through the Opium Wars directly relates to the Western challenge and problems created by the opium trade and British demands (A.1.a).

China's coverage of the 1911 Revolution, Qing decline, and Warlord Era directly addresses China under the late Ch'ing including Western challenges (A.1) and contending forces of westernization and Chinese tradition (A.2).
South Korea's Japanese annexation (1910) relates to Korea's role in the Sino-Japanese War (1894-95) and increasing foreign encroachments (A.2.b).
The United States' role in the U.S. Open Door policy relates to Western responses following the Boxer Rebellion and further Ch'ing concessions (A.2.c).
Imperialism and European expansion over foreign territories directly relate to the Western challenge to China (A.1) and increasing foreign encroachments (A.2.b).
East Asia's modern era of colonialism and WWII Japanese occupation provides broad context for late Qing China (A) and Meiji Japan (B).
Russia's imperial expansion relates to the chilling of Sino-Russian relations during the self-strengthening movement (A.2.a.i).
The United Kingdom's role as the primary Western power in the Opium War relates to British demands for trade advantages and diplomatic parity (A.1.a).
Tokyo's transformation from Edo to the modern imperial capital after the Meiji Restoration relates to the modernization of Japan (B.1).
Trade history and protectionism relate to problems created by the opium trade and British demands for trade advantages (A.1.a).
Asian history's coverage of Qing decline, the Opium War, and Meiji-era modernization provides context for both major sections.
The British Empire's expansion and the handover of Hong Kong connect to British demands culminating in the Opium War (A.1.a).
Colonial expansion and foreign domination relate to the Western challenge to the Ch'ing (A.1) and increasing foreign encroachments on Chinese territory (A.2.b).
